title: Zechariah 13
subtitle: The Fountain for Cleansing and the Stricken Shepherd

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — "A Fountain Will Be Opened for Sin and Impurity"

heb.paragraph: The noun maqor ('fountain, spring') denotes a flowing water source (Jer 2:13; Ps 36:9; Prov 14:27). In Zechariah 13:1, the fountain is 'opened' (niftach, Niphal passive — God opens it) for the specific purpose of cleansing from 'sin and impurity' (chattaʼah veniddah). The term niddah ('impurity') originally denoted menstrual uncleanness (Lev 15:19-20) but expanded to include all forms of ritual defilement. The fountain thus provides perpetual, flowing purification — not a one-time washing but an ongoing source of cleansing.

refs.note: Ezekiel's promise of cleansing water sprinkled on Israel, removing their impurity and giving them a new spirit, provides the closest prophetic parallel to Zechariah's fountain of purification.
Jesus' invitation to drink from the 'rivers of living water' that flow from the believer may echo Zechariah's fountain imagery, connecting the Spirit's gift to the cleansing from sin.
The death penalty for false prophecy — 'a prophet who presumes to speak in my name anything I have not commanded' — is the legal background for the parents' threat in Zechariah 13:3.

notes.note: The fountain for sin and impurity connects directly to the mourning of 12:10-14: repentance over the pierced one leads to cleansing. The passive construction — the fountain 'will be opened' — emphasizes divine initiative: God provides the means of purification that human effort cannot achieve.

The eradication of idols and false prophets from the land fulfills the purification program begun in the night visions (5:1-11). The intensity of the purging is striking: even parents will turn against prophesying children. This is not religious persecution but covenant fidelity: false prophecy is covenant treason, and the community's survival depends on its elimination.

The false prophets' denial of their identity — 'I am a farmer, not a prophet' — is simultaneously comic and tragic. The 'wounds on your body' may be scars from ecstatic self-laceration (1 Kgs 18:28) or disciplinary wounds from parents who tried to stop the false prophesying. The evasive explanation — 'wounds I was given at the house of my friends' — is a transparent lie that only increases the pathos.

Calvin identified the fountain with the blood of Christ, the one cleansing sufficient for all sin. The eradication of false prophecy represents, for Calvin, the triumph of the true Word over all counterfeit spiritual claims. When the true fountain is opened, false sources of guidance lose their appeal and their practitioners are exposed.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term niddah ('impurity') in this context encompasses both ritual and moral defilement. The NET notes observe that the fountain's purpose is comprehensive purification, not merely ceremonial washing.

The 'wounds on your body' (makkot ben yadekha, lit. 'wounds between your hands/arms') is variously interpreted as self-inflicted cuts (ecstatic prophecy), marks of discipline, or simply scars on the chest/back area. The false prophet's evasion — attributing the marks to friendly injury — is deliberately unconvincing.

boda.source: Mark J. Boda, Haggai / Zechariah, NIVAC — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Boda reads the fountain oracle as the 'purification sequel' to the piercing-and-mourning oracle of 12:10-14. The theological sequence is precise: divine initiative (spirit poured out), human response (mourning), and divine provision (fountain opened). The eradication of false prophecy (13:2-6) extends the purification from cultic defilement to epistemological corruption: not only idols but false words about God must be removed. Boda notes that the prophetic office itself is not abolished but purified; the false prophet's denial of prophetic identity reveals that the age of false prophecy has ended.

hist.context: Chapter 13 continues directly from the mourning scene of 12:10-14. The mourning over the pierced one leads to the opening of a cleansing fountain — the sequence is theological: recognition of guilt (12:10), repentance (12:11-14), and purification (13:1). The fountain is opened specifically for 'the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem,' the same group that received the spirit of grace in 12:10. The oracle then addresses the eradication of idolatry and false prophecy from the land (13:2-6). The false prophets will be so thoroughly discredited that they will deny their prophetic identity: 'I am not a prophet; I am a farmer' (13:5). Even their own parents will condemn any child who falsely prophesies, invoking the death penalty of Deuteronomy 18:20. The cryptic reference to 'wounds on your body' (13:6) likely alludes to self-laceration practiced by ecstatic false prophets (cf. 1 Kgs 18:28).

sections.header: Verses 7–9 — "Strike the Shepherd, and the Sheep Will Be Scattered"

heb.gloss: my associate, my companion, my close one
heb.paragraph: The word ʼamit (from ʼam, 'kinsman, fellow') describes the stricken shepherd as 'the man who is close to me' (gever ʼamiti) — God's intimate associate. This is the strongest possible term for relational closeness: the shepherd is not merely God's employee but His peer, His companion. The term appears elsewhere only in Leviticus (Lev 6:2; 18:20; 19:15, 17; 24:19; 25:14-15, 17) where it denotes a fellow member of the covenant community. Applied to the shepherd-messiah, it asserts a unique relationship between this figure and God — a relationship that the NT identifies with the divine-human unity of Christ.

heb.gloss: to refine, to smelt, to test by fire
heb.paragraph: The verb tsaraf describes the metallurgical process of refining precious metals by heating them until impurities separate and can be removed (Mal 3:2-3; Ps 66:10; Isa 48:10). In 13:9, the surviving remnant is refined 'like silver' and tested 'like gold' — the most precious metals requiring the most intense heat. The refining is not punitive but purificatory: its purpose is to produce a people who can genuinely say 'The LORD is our God' and to whom God responds, 'They are my people.'

refs.note: Jesus quotes Zechariah 13:7 on the Mount of Olives before His arrest: 'I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered,' identifying Himself as the stricken shepherd and the disciples as the scattered sheep.

Mark's parallel citation emphasizes that Jesus understood His passion as the fulfillment of Zechariah's shepherd prophecy, making the scattering of the disciples a necessary, prophesied consequence of the shepherd's striking.
Peter's description of faith 'refined by fire' and 'proved genuine' echoes Zechariah's metallurgical imagery of the remnant refined like silver and tested like gold.

notes.note: The staggering element of this verse is that God commands the sword to strike His own shepherd — 'the man close to me.' This is not the worthless shepherd of 11:17 but the good shepherd, God's intimate associate. The striking is divinely orchestrated, not a tragic accident. This can only be understood christologically: God Himself initiates the suffering of His Son for the redemptive purpose of refining His people.

The proportions — two-thirds perish, one-third survives — describe a severe but not total judgment. The refining metaphor transforms the remnant from survivors into precious metal: they enter the fire as mixed ore and emerge as pure silver and gold. The covenant formula that closes the passage (13:9b) is the ultimate goal of all of Zechariah's shepherd theology: a people who truly belong to God and a God who truly claims them.

Calvin identified the stricken shepherd as Christ, noting that God's command to 'strike the shepherd' parallels Isaiah 53:10: 'It was the LORD's will to crush him and cause him to suffer.' The suffering is voluntary, purposeful, and redemptive. The refining of the remnant through fire represents the sanctification that flows from Christ's atoning work: the cross is the furnace in which the church is purified.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term gever ('man, strong man') used for the shepherd is more emphatic than the common ʼish ('man'). Combined with ʼamiti ('my associate'), it describes a figure of significant stature and intimate divine relationship. The NET notes observe that this cannot refer to the worthless shepherd of 11:17, who is God's enemy, not His associate.

The covenant formula — 'I will say, "They are my people"' — echoes Hosea 2:23 and Ezekiel 37:23, where the same formula marks the climactic restoration of the covenant relationship after judgment and purification.

boda.source: Mark J. Boda, Haggai / Zechariah, NIVAC — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Boda reads the stricken shepherd oracle as the 'redemptive suffering climax' of Zechariah's shepherd theology. The trajectory runs from the rejected shepherd of chapter 11 (who is insulted and abandoned) to the stricken shepherd of chapter 13 (who is struck down by God's own command). The shepherd described as God's ʼamit ('associate, companion') stands in the closest possible relationship to God — a relationship that makes the striking all the more shocking. The refining metaphor (13:9) transforms catastrophe into purification: the remnant that survives the fire emerges into renewed covenantal communion, completing the book's restoration arc.

hist.context: Zechariah 13:7-9 contains the most quoted shepherd oracle in the New Testament. God Himself commands the sword to 'strike the shepherd' — the man who is 'close to me' (God's intimate associate). The striking scatters the sheep, and God turns His hand against 'the little ones' (hatseʼoirim, the vulnerable members of the flock). Two-thirds of the people will perish, but the surviving third will be refined through fire like precious metal. The result is the covenant formula in its purest form: 'They will call on my name and I will answer them. I will say, "They are my people," and they will say, "The LORD is our God"' (13:9). The passage is difficult because God appears to orchestrate the suffering: He commands the sword, He strikes the shepherd, He turns against the little ones. But the purpose is purificatory, not merely punitive: the fire produces refined silver and gold, and the remnant emerges into renewed covenant relationship. Jesus directly applies 13:7 to His own arrest and the disciples' scattering (Matt 26:31; Mark 14:27).

themes.note: Chapter 13 centers on Holiness (the fountain for cleansing, the purging of false prophecy, the refining fire), Covenant (the climactic covenant formula of 13:9), Judgment (the striking of the shepherd and the two-thirds perishing), and Prophecy (the shepherd oracle fulfilled in Christ's passion).

rows.label: vv.1-6
rows.text: Fountain for cleansing opened; eradication of idolatry and false prophecy from the purified land

rows.label: vv.7-9
rows.text: The stricken shepherd: God strikes His associate, sheep scattered, remnant refined like precious metal into covenant renewal

lit.note: Chapter 13 completes the mourning-cleansing-refining sequence: the pierced one of 12:10 opens a fountain for sin (13:1), false prophecy is purged (13:2-6), the shepherd is struck by God's own sword (13:7), and the refined remnant confesses the covenant formula (13:9).

ppl.name: Zechariah
ppl.text: Zechariah 13:7-9 contains the most quoted shepherd oracle in the New Testament…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Fountain for Cleansing and the Stricken Shepherd

translations.text: This third I will put into the fire; I will refine them like silver and test them like gold. They will call on my name and I will answer them; I will say, ‘They are my people,’ and they will say, ‘The Lord is our God.’ ”

And I will put this third into the fire, and refine them as one refines silver, and test them as gold is tested. They will call upon my name, and I will answer them. I will say, ‘They are my people’; and they will say, ‘The LORD is my God.’”

And if anyone still prophesies, their father and mother, to whom they were born, will say to them, ‘You must die, because you have told lies in the Lord’s name.’ Then their own parents will stab the one who prophesies.

And if anyone again prophesies, his father and mother who bore him will say to him, ‘You shall not live, for you speak lies in the name of the LORD.’ And his father and mother who bore him shall pierce him through when he prophesies.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Fountain for Cleansing and the Stricken Shepherd within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

coaching.tip: 'On that day a fountain shall be opened... to cleanse them from sin and uncleanness' (v. 1). The cleansing is permanent — an open fountain, not a closed cistern. This picks up the 'single day' sin removal of 3:9. Zechariah layers purification images: reclothing, fountain, refining fire. Sin requires comprehensive treatment.

'Strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered' (v. 7). Jesus quotes this at Gethsemane (Matt 26:31). The shepherd is 'the man who stands next to me' — God's intimate associate. When this shepherd is struck, God's own flock scatters. The disciples' flight fulfills Zechariah. Prophetic script becomes passion narrative.

prayer_prompt: As a fountain is opened for sin and impurity and the shepherd is struck so the sheep scatter, consider asking God whether you have fully entered the cleansing He has already provided — or are standing at the edge of it.
